questions.questionText: The most common site for a peptic ulcer is the:
questions.explanation: Duodenal ulcers are the most common type and are highly associated with H. pylori.

questions.questionText: A defining histological feature of Celiac disease (Gluten-sensitive enteropathy) is:
questions.explanation: Immune reaction to gliadin results in destruction of the absorptive villi.

questions.optionA: Mucosal inflammation beginning in the rectum
questions.optionD: Continuous involvement of the left colon
questions.explanation: Transmural inflammation leads to strictures, fistulas, and a 'cobblestone' mucosa.
UC starts in the rectum and spreads continuously proximally, limited to the mucosa/submucosa.

questions.questionText: A key complication of chronic Ulcerative Colitis is a high risk for developing:
questions.explanation: Long-standing UC has a significantly increased risk of dysplasia and colon cancer.

questions.questionText: Hirschsprung disease (Congenital aganglionic megacolon) is caused by a failure of:
questions.optionA: Neural crest cells to migrate into the distal bowel
questions.explanation: Absence of Meissner's and Auerbach's plexuses causes a functional obstruction.

questions.questionText: The most common precursor lesion to colorectal cancer is the:
questions.explanation: Most colorectal carcinomas arise from adenomas via the adenoma-carcinoma sequence.

questions.questionText: Familial Adenomatous Polyposis (FAP) is caused by a mutation in the:
questions.explanation: FAP patients develop hundreds of polyps; malignant transformation is 100%.

questions.questionText: A characteristic finding in Acute Appendicitis is:
questions.optionB: Neutrophilic infiltration of the muscularis propria

questions.questionText: Zollinger-Ellison syndrome is caused by a Gastrin-secreting tumor, most often located in the:
questions.explanation: Gastrinomas cause massive acid hypersecretion leading to refractory peptic ulcers.

questions.questionText: In GI Pathology, which statement is most accurate regarding the core definition and diagnostic concept?
questions.optionA: It reflects the most exam-relevant definition used in standard Pathology texts
questions.optionB: It is an obsolete definition no longer used in clinical practice
questions.optionC: It is used only in pediatric population and never in adults
questions.optionD: It has no diagnostic significance in NEET-PG pattern questions
questions.explanation: This tests foundational definition-level knowledge from standard Pathology references for GI Pathology. Subject: Pathology. Chapter: GI Pathology.

questions.questionText: The most important etiology/risk factor to suspect first in a typical GI Pathology scenario is:
questions.optionA: The common high-yield risk factor repeatedly asked in entrance exams
questions.optionB: A rare cause seen only in isolated case reports
questions.optionD: An etiology excluded by routine first-line workup
questions.explanation: NEET-PG and INI-CET focus on common, high-yield causes before rare differentials. Subject: Pathology. Chapter: GI Pathology.
